title: Multi-threaded simulation can be non-deterministic with racy input
body: This is kind of a duplicate of #3359 / #3360, but I'm filing a new one rather than re-opening to focus the discussion. In #3359 / #3360 we didn't really confirm whether we are happy to live with this. --- Consider this minimal example, which contains a race condition between the two always blocks (value of `b` depends on the execution order of the 2 blocks): ```systemverilog module top(clk, a, b); input clk; wire logic clk; output a; int a = 0; output b; int b = 0; always @(posedge clk) begin a = a + 1; end always @(posedge clk) begin b <= a + 1; end endmodule ``` The MTask graph looks like this: (This is after the 'FixDataHazards' step, but the final one is functionally the same). 'mt2' writes `a`, 'mt4' reads `a`, but they have no defined ordering, and if they end up on different threads, the multi-threaded execution will be non-deterministic at run-time (that is, depending on thread scheduling, you will get different results on different runs). This seems pretty bad, as re-running the same simulation can produce different results. --- t_flag_csplit has such a race and it's output is hence non-deterministic. Run this inside `test_regress/`: ``` while true; do ./t/t_flag_csplit.py --vltmt &> /dev/null && md5sum obj_vltmt/t_flag_csplit/simx.vcd ; done ``` And in another terminal: ``` stress -m $(nproc) ``` You will see 2 different MD5 values reported --- To be clear, this has been the behaviour in multi-threaded mode forever. `FixDataHazards` in V3OrderParallel.cpp intended to fix these up, but it never did due to the bug described in #3359, which was there from day 1. --- I can of course just fix `t_flag_csplit`, but non-determinism seems pretty bad for the user. (Ideally we should help them find races, but in a more direct way.) I'm leaning towards fixing `FixDataHazards` to insert just enough serialization for these R/W hazards to be made deterministic. Note we originally didn't merge #3360, because it had a big negative performance impact (the constraint can be more relaxed than that), but that fix in itself is not sufficient either (the OrderGraph does not encode some of these dependencies). I would appreciate your thoughts. Should I proceed? Or do we want to continue living with accepting non-deterministic simulation if the user design is racy? Should this be under an option (hard to test)? What performance penalty are we willing to accept?
